entry.title: Working at startups!
entry.content:
The first thing that might bring to most peoples minds when they hear the word ‘startup’ is a cool, vibrant environment with casual dress code, bean bags, etc. Although all of this is true for most startups, there is more to it than its ‘cool culture’. Whatever I share in this blog post would be from my experience of working with technology startups as a Software Engineer. This may or may not apply to non tech startups and/or to non technology roles.

After my internship at IIT Bombay, I was looking for a job. I didn’t have a job from campus placement as I didn’t sit for placements (It’s a story for another day). I applied off campus & got a job offer from a service based MNC. I was looking forward to accept the offer as I didn’t have anything better at the time. It took some serious brain washing from my friends (especially the ones who had already joined a service based MNC and were regretting) to stop me from accepting the job offer.

“What would I do?” I thought.

A friend of mine who was already working for a startup suggested me to start applying to startups as they would pay better and I’ll get to learn more. I did some googling and found out about the best platforms to apply for startup jobs in India. I then created a profile on Angel list and started applying to startups.

Within 2 weeks I was able to get 2-3 job offers from startups in Mumbai & Pune. And that is how I started my journey of working with startups!

Interview Process

To be honest, there is no fixed interview process when it comes to startups. I have seen startups who interview for 4-5 rounds rigorously and I have also seen startups who give out job offers without any formal interview process. The second kinds discuss some high level things about past projects, experience in a particular technology, etc.

But with most of the startups, the thing that I found common is the assignment. More than 50% startups that I interviewed with gave me some kind of assignment which required me to build an application to solve a problem in a span of 3 to 5 days.

The assignment ranged from very easy to medium difficulty levels. A few required only to build a simple CRUD application to store user data or something similar while there were others who were focusing on building full stack application with good code quality, deployment on a real time server & database, etc. The difficulty & scope of the assignment also depends on the profile to which you are applying.

For example: A Backend Engineer role would require you to build a set of APIs, connect it to a database & implement some kind of business logic & validations for a given problem statement, writing API documentation using tools like Swagger, etc.

A Full Stack Engineer role would require you to do everything you’d do for a backend engineer role along with connecting the backend with a front end framework or library like Reactjs, Angularjs or something similar, deploying the application using a cloud service provider like Heroku or Amazon Web Services, etc.

A few startups would accept your assignment in any technology as long as you solve the given problem while there are others who are very particular about the technology stack that you are choosing.

Learning & Growth

This is an area where I feel startups outshine any other type of organization. If you are a technical nerd and want to grow at rocket speed then I don’t think there could be any place better than a startup. If you are someone isn’t very passionate about taking on challenging work with strict deadlines & lack confidence when it comes to your tech skills then a startup may not be an ideal place for you.

If you have entrepreneurial ambitions then startups give you a very close look at how different operations are run and how different teams such as design, front end, back end, operations, finance, marketing work together.

The open culture of startups allow you to get a close look & learn from different teams. Also smaller teams mean you will almost always be part of meetings where important business decisions will be taken, marketing plans will be decided, etc. I have found the non technical knowledge gained from startups as useful as the technical skills.

Compensation

Again this point is very similar to the ‘Interview Process’ point. There is no defined range of salaries that startups offer. When we talk about service based companies like Infosys, TCS, Wipro, etc there is a range in which they offer salaries to a fresh graduate engineer. Fortunately this is not the case with startups.

There is a common misconception that I have seen in students where they feel startups pay lower salaries than MNCs. There are startups who pay lower than even these service based companies and there are ones who pay higher than even what investment banking firms pay to engineers. Compensation in startups directly depends on your skills, profile, previous internships experience, projects and many other such factors which prove that you are a valuable asset to have on the team.

Generally, early stage startups with no or low seed funding pay quite less while well funded startups tend to pay much better for obvious reasons. A lot of startups also offer equity to initial employees which could be really profitable in the long run if the startup works out really well.

Work Life Balance

This is an area where MNCs outmatch startups. There is little to very low work life balance when it comes to most of the startups. The learning and growth that you receive could be at the cost of your health, mental peace, lesser time spent with family/friends, etc. This is not to demotivate anyone but I wanted to give a fair assessment of startups in as unbiased way as possible.

A lot of startups have working saturdays, no fixed office hours, etc. Startups are not a 9 to 5 job and you would struggle here if you treat it like one. It’s very easy to burn out and give up.

A few pointers for people interested in working for startups

1) Work on your skills before you apply. Working for startups is a roller coaster ride where you might not get time to work on your skills. Be prepared with at least more than basic knowledge of your technology stack.

2) Choose a company wisely. Read their glassdoor reviews if available. Talk to the employer clearly about your expectations, the work that you’ll be getting or any other question that you might have before you accept a job offer.

3) Choose learning over money. Decisions made solely on money are seldom right. In the initial years at least, it’s more important to arm yourself with knowledge & skills than money. Money and learning opportunity need not be mutually exclusive, but when it is, I’d rather choose learning.

4) Choose positions which give you major responsibilities & ownership of the work. No matter how many personal projects you have worked on, nothing teaches you more than writing software for production systems.

5) Treat your technology stack or programming languages like your friends and not like your wife, you can always have more than one. You should be open to learn new technologies, programming languages, frameworks, etc if required. I have seen far too many people being obsessed with a particular technology and in turn turning down good opportunities.

6) Put your projects on Github and be active on communities like LinkedIn. Have a strong profile on Angel List, Instahyre, Github, LinkedIn, etc. Human Resources and recruitment teams are always looking out for talented people on these platforms.

7) If possible, always try to have more than 1 job offer in hand. It not only gives you more options with which you can make better decisions but also helps you with things like salary negotiations.
entry.summary: The first thing that might bring to most peoples minds when they hear the word ‘startup’ is a cool, vibrant environment with casual dress code, bean bags, etc. Although all of this is true for most startups, there is more to it than its ‘cool culture’. Whatever I share in this blog post would be from my experience of working with technology startups as a Software Engineer. This may or may not apply to non tech startups and/or to non technology roles.

entry.title: Lets Go!
entry.content:
It’d been a while since I had learnt or tried any new programming language. The last programming language I learnt was Python which was over 2.5 years ago! Python started out as a hobby to hack around with some cool scripts to automate tasks.

These days I primarily work with JavaScript/Node.js along with Python.

Last weekend I decided to try the Google’s famous programming lanugage Go!

Go has been built for scale and is expected to be a very strong choice in enterprise space in near future! Being a compiled language it really out performs popular interpreted languages such as Python and JavaScript which are heavily used in backend development.

Go could be a good alternative to the above programming languages especially if your application requires high computation on the backend. It brings out the accuracy of statically typed languages and ease of dynamically typed languages.

I roughly spent 15 hours on learning Go in 1 week and wrote about 15-20 small programs experimenting with the basic syntax of the language such as data types, conditions, functions, looping along with some data structures like slices, maps, etc.

I started with sentdex’s tutorials and watched about 12-14 video lectures. I then refered to the famous Go blog gobyexample.com to look up at examples and play around with them on my own. I also watched a 1 hour YouTube video on the language explaining all the basic concepts quickly.

Learning syntax is important but not enough to really understand a programming language. I am a strong believer in learning by doing. So I decided to build some simple applications using Go to improve my understanding of the language.

I built some simple web services using it such as a simple echo server, services interacting with Google APIs to return some search results to the user in JSON format, etc.

My overall experience with the language was very solid. As I have used C and C++ extensively (for 4-5 years) in past for competitive programming and for solving problems on algorithms and data structures, I could quickly grasp the basic syntax in a couple of hours.

A few things that struck me as unique/different/amazing about Go are:

1) If there are any unused imports or variables, your program won’t run. This is a good practice from software quality point of view but could be really annoying if you are quickly trying to hack some programs!

2) Go feels more restrictive overall and strict about the style/pattern with which you write code. For example: You cannot write else or else if on new lines, they need to adhere to the coding style recommended by Google/Go.

3) It might take some time to wrap your head around a few things at first, for example here is a code in Go to initialize an array of size 5 with numbers 1 to 5.

We use := if we are initializing and declaring a variable at the same time.

It is much more intuitive in C++ with:

It’s easier to understand such things if you look at them through generic ways like:

<name> := [<size>] <data_type> {values1, value2}

4) You can create functions in Go that can return multiple values which is not possible in most of the programming languages.

Here function vals returns 2 integers.

5) You can create functions in Go that can accept any number of arguments. These functions are called variadic functions.

Example of Variadic functions

We can call the above function with any number of arguments (numbers) & it will print the total sum of all the values.

6) Go feels incredibly simple to learn, especially if you come from C/C++ background. It’s like C and Python decided to have a baby. Its simplicity can be naively demonstrated by its list of keywords.

List of all the keywords in Go

Compare this with something like Java which is a widely used language in enterprise space & you will get an idea.

List of all the keywords in Java

Obviously the comparison isn’t completely fair as Go has been around just for a decade now and Java for a much more longer time. With new revisions, Java added features & keywords but it still gives us a rough idea about the gentle learning curve for Go.

7) Goroutines make it possible to write non blocking code in synchronous manner. This is a huge advantage as you do not have to deal with things like callback, callback hell, etc which are available in programming languages like JavaScript. The Go runtime handles these things for you efficiently. Go being designed to be used internally at Google has managed to handle concurrency in a very efficient way.

Link to Github Repository of some Go programs: Go Programs

entry.title: How a random conversation on a train led to a job offer!

link.title: How a random conversation on a train led to a job offer!

entry.content:
It was 1st of November 2018 & I was looking for a job switch. After work, I boarded a train from Santacruz and fortunately got a seat.

A gentleman (lets call him X) probably in his early 50s, sitting next to me on the train was watching a JavaScript video on Prototypes on his phone. After a bit of hesitation, (I am not very good at striking conversations) I decided to talk to him.

He was one of the co-founders and a CTO of an early stage startup in Mumbai. As I come from a startup too, we had a lot in common to discuss. We discussed a lot of things from technology to startups, from books to blogs. He had also worked with JP Morgan for more than a decade on ETL systems so it was interesting to listen to his experience on that. It was a good conversation.

After X got off the train when his destination arrived, someone else said “Hello” from behind. I looked around, a man in his 30s was looking at me. I replied “Hello”. He quickly asked me “Are you a JavaScript developer?”. After I replied positively he asked “Are you looking for a job switch?”

I said “Yes!”.

I was still clueless and then I realized that he had overheard my conversation with X.

He handed over his business card to me. He was heading the Data Science team in one of the well funded and fast growing startups in Mumbai. He told me his company is looking for some JavaScript developers and asked me to mail him my resume and other details. I thanked him and he got down at the next station.

I mailed him my resume the next day and he said he will forward it internally to the recruiters and will get back to me in a couple of days. We exchanged a few emails after that where he asked me about my preferences in technology stack & asked me to try out their product to get a better understanding about what they’re trying to build.

I got a reply from him in a couple of days that someone from the Human Resources team will contact me and schedule an interview.

I got my telephonic interview scheduled on a Saturday. I was asked some basic questions on different topics such as REST Architecture, Node.js, Python v/s Node.js, Django v/s Express and other similar questions. The interview lasted for about 45 minutes.

I was immediately informed that I have cleared the telephonic interview and will be given a programming assignment as the next step in the interview process. I had to complete the assignment in 3-4 days.

The assignment required me to build a complete full stack application(prototype), pushing the code to Github with frequent commits & deploying it live on a server.

It was a challenge for me to complete the assignment in 3-4 days as I was already working as a full time Software Engineer in a high paced startup. Also the problem statement was completely new to me so there were a lot of things I needed to figure out. Somehow after spending a couple of hours every day after work, I managed to complete the application in time!

I built the application using Node.js, Express.js for the backend APIs, Vanilla JavaScript, Bootstrap & some other libraries for the front end, MongoDB as my database which I hosted on a remote Atlas Server. I finally deployed the application on Heroku.

After submitting the application I got a call for the final interview in their Mumbai office.

My final interview was scheduled on the upcoming friday. I was going to be interviewed by one of their senior full stack developers. The final interview consisted of a brief discussion on the programming assignment from the previous round, some improvements that could be added on the top of existing code & other discussion on Node.js, JavaScript, some problems on Algorithms & Data Structures.

It was one of the best & longest interviews I have ever had. The interview was supposed to last for an hour but it ended up being a 2 hour interview! The interviewer was very excited to know about my background in Algorithms & Programming Competitions so he threw a lot of algorithmic problems at me.

We went on to discuss some practical applications of the algorithms we were discussing. He challenged me with some new problems which required me to use the standard data structures in interesting ways. I was asked to implement some data structures and explain the time complexities of different operations on them.

It felt more like a deep technical discussion between 2 engineers than an interview. In fact in one of the discussions on time complexitiy analysis the interviewer made an error & I corrected him. He quickly realized it & humbly accepted it. In the end, the interviewer realized that we had spent far too much time with this interview so unfortunately we had to stop our discussion there.

After a couple of days after the final interview, I was given a job offer.

I didn’t end up joining there as I had other offers in hand but it ended up being an interesting experience to share!
entry.summary: It was 1st of November 2018 & I was looking for a job switch. After work, I boarded a train from Santacruz and fortunately got a seat.
